VOR & ILS.clue: This is the navigation information a VOR provides to a pilot.
choices: Distance to the station in nautical miles
The aircraft's altitude above the station
The magnetic radial from the station
A voice broadcast of the station's weather
VOR & ILS.explanation: VOR provides bearing — the radial from the station — not distance, altitude, or weather. Distance is provided by DME (Distance Measuring Equipment), often collocated with a VOR. Weather broadcasts are a separate service.

VOR & ILS.clue: This is the function of the Omni-Bearing Selector (OBS) on a VOR indicator.
choices: To tune the VOR station's frequency
To display the aircraft's current altitude
To control the VOR station's signal strength
To select a specific radial to or from the VOR station
VOR & ILS.explanation: The OBS selects the desired radial. The CDI then shows deviation from that radial. Station tuning is done at the nav radio, not the OBS. The OBS does not display altitude. Pilots cannot control the station's transmitted signal strength.

VOR & ILS.clue: This is the system that provides both vertical and horizontal guidance to pilots during landing approaches.
choices: VOR
ADF/NDB
DME
ILS (Inst
VOR & ILS.explanation: The Instrument Landing System (ILS) combines the localizer (horizontal, centered on the runway) with the glideslope (vertical descent guidance) for precision approaches. VOR and ADF provide bearing only, not vertical guidance. DME provides distance, not guidance.

VOR & ILS.clue: This is what a marker beacon provides during an ILS approach.
choices: Horizontal course guidance along the runway centerline
Position fixes at defined points on the approach path
Vertical descent guidance from an ILS ground antenna
Voice broadcast of runway surface conditions in use
VOR & ILS.explanation: Marker beacons provide position fixes — the aircraft overflies them at known points along the approach. Horizontal guidance comes from the localizer, vertical guidance from the glideslope, and voice broadcasts come from ATIS or AWOS.

VOR & ILS.clue: This is the information DME provides to the pilot.
choices: Slant-range distance from the aircraft to the ground station
The current magnetic bearing from the selected station
The current weather observation at the station
The current runway heading at the destination airport
VOR & ILS.explanation: DME provides distance — specifically slant-range, not horizontal distance. Bearing comes from VOR. Weather is an ATIS/AWOS broadcast. Runway information is from the approach chart, not DME.

Transponders & ADS-B.clue: This transponder mode transmits both the four-digit identification code and the aircraft's pressure altitude.
choices: Mode A
Mode S only
Mode C
No mode transmits altitude
Transponders & ADS-B.explanation: Mode C adds altitude reporting to the identification code Mode A already provides. Mode S is a more advanced standard that includes everything Mode C does plus unique addressing and data link; however, the specific mode that FIRST added altitude reporting to the basic ID function is Mode C.

Transponders & ADS-B.clue: This is the function of the 24-bit address in a Mode S transponder.
choices: It uniquely identifies the airframe and enables
It encodes the current squawk code
It indicates the transponder's data rate
It stores the aircraft's GPS position
Transponders & ADS-B.explanation: The 24-bit ICAO address is a unique airframe identifier. The squawk code is a separate four-digit pilot-settable code. Transponder data rate is fixed by the standard. GPS position comes from the GPS receiver, not the transponder address.

Transponders & ADS-B.clue: According to FAR 91.413, this is how often an aircraft transponder must be tested and inspected.
choices: Every 12 calendar months
Every 36 calendar months
Every 24 calendar months
Every 100 flight hours
Transponders & ADS-B.explanation: FAR 91.413 requires transponder test and inspection at least every 24 calendar months. Annual (12-month) and 36-month intervals are not the required cadence. Flight-hour intervals do not apply to this requirement.

Transponders & ADS-B.clue: The transponder squawk code is set by the pilot to do this.
choices: Select the transponder's operating frequency
Determine the aircraft's altitude reporting resolution
Program the 24-bit Mode S address
Identify the aircraft to ATC, often with controller-assigned codes
Transponders & ADS-B.explanation: The squawk code identifies the aircraft to ATC. Controllers assign unique codes to track flights individually. Reserved codes communicate specific conditions (emergency, radio failure, hijack). Transponder frequency is fixed by the standard. Altitude reporting resolution is also fixed. The 24-bit address is a separate, permanent Mode S identifier.

Transponders & ADS-B.clue: This is what ADS-B Out broadcasts to ATC and other aircraft.
choices: Only the aircraft's ICAO address
GPS-derived position, velocity
The pilot's voice transmissions
Weather radar imagery
Transponders & ADS-B.explanation: ADS-B Out is a continuous broadcast of position, velocity, altitude, and identification data. It is not voice communication and does not carry weather radar imagery — those are separate systems.

Antennas & Installation.clue: This is the typical mounting location for an aircraft transponder antenna.
choices: On the top of the vertical stabilizer
On the leading edge of the wing
Inside the cockpit next to the pilot
On the underside (belly) of the aircraft
Antennas & Installation.explanation: Transponder antennas go on the belly because ATC radar is below the aircraft. Vertical stabilizer and wing leading-edge locations are used for other antennas (VOR, nav/strobe) but not transponders. The antenna cannot be inside the cockpit — the airframe would block the signal.

Antennas & Installation.clue: This is the optimal mounting location for a VHF communication antenna on a GA aircraft.
choices: On the fuselage (top or bottom) with a clear line
Inside the cockpit near the pilot
Attached to the propeller hub
On the inside surface of the windshield
Antennas & Installation.explanation: Fuselage mounting (top or bottom) with clear line of sight and proper ground plane is the correct approach. Interior mounting is shielded by the airframe. Propeller mounting is unworkable. Windshield mounting lacks a ground plane.

Antennas & Installation.clue: When replacing a coaxial cable for a communication antenna, this is CRUCIAL.
choices: Use any coax available, regardless of type
Splice multiple shorter coax runs together to save cost
Ignore bend radius as long as the cable fits
Match the impedance (usually 50 Ω) and use correct connectors
Antennas & Installation.explanation: Impedance match and correct cable selection are fundamental. Using the wrong impedance (75 ohm video coax instead of 50 ohm comm coax, for example) causes reflections and power loss. Splicing and ignoring bend radius both introduce losses and potential damage that degrade antenna system performance.

Antennas & Installation.clue: This is what regular inspection of aircraft communication antennas should include.
choices: Only the connector at the avionics rack end of the coaxial cable
Replacing the antenna every 12 months regardless of condition
Adjusting the antenna's transmit frequency
Checking for physical damage, base corrosion, mounting hardware condition, sealant
Antennas & Installation.explanation: Antenna inspection covers physical, mounting, and bonding aspects. Only checking the far-end connector misses the antenna itself. Antennas are replaced when inspection reveals damage, not on a fixed interval. Antennas do not have tunable transmit frequencies — they are passive radiators.

Antennas & Installation.clue: This is the typical mounting location of an ILS glideslope antenna on a general aviation aircraft.
choices: Belly under the main gear
Top of the vertical stabilizer
Underside of a wingtip
Forward fuselage near the nose
Antennas & Installation.explanation: The glideslope antenna mounts on the forward nose of the aircraft for unobstructed forward and downward line-of-sight to the airport's glideslope transmitter during approach. Belly mounting is blocked by landing gear, tail mounting blocks forward signal.

VHF & Audio.clue: In general-aviation aircraft audio systems, this describes typical 'mono' audio use.
choices: Only one earpiece of the headset receives audio; the other is silent
Mono refers to audio broadcast on AM only
The same audio signal is sent to both earpieces of the headset
Mono audio is obsolete and no longer used
VHF & Audio.explanation: Mono audio sends the same signal to both ears — standard for voice traffic that does not benefit from stereo imaging. One-ear-only is not the definition of mono. Mono is not tied to AM broadcast. It remains current practice in GA audio panels.

VHF & Audio.clue: This is the primary purpose of an audio control panel in an aircraft.
choices: To boost the transmit power of the VHF radio
To allow the pilot to select which radios to transmit
To convert AC power for avionics use
To regulate cabin pressure
VHF & Audio.explanation: The audio panel is a source-selection and routing device. It does not amplify transmit power (that is the radio's transmitter), convert power, or regulate pressurization.

VHF & Audio.clue: This is a common cause of intermittent or noisy audio in an aircraft communication system.
choices: Poor or broken grounding, corroded connectors, broken
The radio is tuned to the wrong frequency
The pilot is wearing the wrong type of headset
The audio panel has too many sources selected
VHF & Audio.explanation: Intermittent/noisy audio is typically a physical connection problem — grounding, connectors, shields, or jack contacts. Wrong frequency would produce no received audio at all, not noise. Headset type and source selection are secondary factors.

VHF & Audio.clue: This is what the squelch control does in a VHF radio system.
choices: Boosts the transmit power of the radio
Selects the operating frequency
Controls the audio panel's intercom volume
Suppresses the receiver audio output during periods
VHF & Audio.explanation: Squelch mutes the receiver audio when there is no signal (or signal below threshold), preventing constant background hiss. It does not affect transmit power, frequency selection, or intercom functions.

VHF & Audio.clue: This is the frequency range used by aviation VHF voice communications.
choices: 190 to 1750 kHz
960 to 1215 MHz
VHF & Audio.explanation: Aviation VHF voice comms operate 118.000 to 136.975 MHz with 25 kHz (or 8.33 kHz in Europe) channel spacing. 108-117.975 MHz is the VOR and ILS band. 190-1750 kHz is NDB. 960-1215 MHz is DME.

GPS & Advanced Surveillance.clue: This is the minimum number of satellites a GPS receiver must track for a three-dimensional position fix.
GPS & Advanced Surveillance.explanation: Four satellites are the minimum for a 3D fix — three for spatial triangulation and a fourth to resolve receiver clock bias. Two satellites cannot produce a position fix. Three can produce only a 2D fix (assuming known altitude). Eight is typical for modern receivers tracking more than the minimum, but it is not the minimum requirement.

GPS & Advanced Surveillance.clue: This is what the acronym WAAS stands for.
choices: Wireless Altitude Assurance Source
Wide Angle Approach Sensor
Weather Augmentation Assistance System
Wide Area Augmentation System
GPS & Advanced Surveillance.explanation: WAAS is the Wide Area Augmentation System — FAA-developed GPS augmentation for improved accuracy and integrity in North America.

GPS & Advanced Surveillance.clue: Compared to VOR, this describes ADF/NDB navigation in general aviation.
choices: Generally less accurate and less reliable than VOR
More accurate than VOR under all conditions
Requires GPS augmentation to function
Only used for precision approaches
GPS & Advanced Surveillance.explanation: ADF/NDB's low-frequency signal is more susceptible to interference than VOR's VHF signal. VOR is the more accurate and reliable navigation aid. ADF does not need GPS and is used for non-precision approaches and en route navigation, not precision approaches.

GPS & Advanced Surveillance.clue: This is what the acronym TCAS stands for.
choices: Tactical Combat Avoidance Sensor
Traffic Collision Avoidance System
Terminal Control Approach Structure
Thrust Control Activation Subsystem
GPS & Advanced Surveillance.explanation: TCAS is Traffic Collision Avoidance System (sometimes expanded as Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System).

GPS & Advanced Surveillance.clue: This is the primary function of ADS-B In on a general aviation aircraft.
choices: Receives traffic and weather data
Broadcasts own position to ATC
Interrogates other transponders
Generates pressure altitude for the transponder
GPS & Advanced Surveillance.explanation: ADS-B In receives Traffic Information Service-Broadcast (TIS-B) and Flight Information Service-Broadcast (FIS-B) data from ground stations and other aircraft. ADS-B Out handles the transmit side. Mode S transponders perform interrogations; the blind encoder generates pressure altitude.

Extended Coverage 1.clue: A WAAS-enabled GPS receiver still requires this many satellites to compute a three-dimensional navigation fix.
choices: Two satellites
Four satellites
Three satellites
Eight satellites
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: WAAS is an augmentation overlay that improves accuracy and integrity — it does not change the basic geometry requirement. A receiver still needs four GPS satellites (three for triangulation, a fourth to resolve clock bias) to get a 3D fix. WAAS then applies differential corrections received from GEO satellites on top of that four-satellite solution.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: How many GPS satellites must a receiver track for 3D navigation with WAAS?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: A pilot hears only a continuous carrier tone on the comm frequency rather than a reply from ATC; this is the scenario most likely to blame.
choices: The aircraft transponder has failed completely in Mode C reply
The aircraft's altitude encoder is miscalibrated and reading wrong
The ADS-B Out ground receiver station has lost all of its own power
A push-to-talk switch on the frequency is stuck in the keyed position
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: A continuous tone on an otherwise-working comm frequency is the textbook signature of a stuck microphone — a PTT switch stuck keyed somewhere on the frequency, transmitting a continuous carrier and blocking the channel. Transponder, ADS-B, and encoder faults affect their own systems and do not produce a comm-frequency carrier.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: A pilot hears a constant tone instead of a response from ATC.. Which scenario is most likely?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: The antenna used for VOR reception on a piston single is usually mounted in this physical fashion.
choices: Vertical blade antennas mounted on the underside of the fuselage
Internal loop antennas installed inside the cabin for electromagnetic shielding
Horizontally polarized cat-whisker or blade on the vertical stabilizer
Helical antennas mounted at each wing tip for polarization diversity
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: VOR ground transmitters emit horizontally polarized signals, so airborne VOR antennas are horizontally polarized dipoles — typically the dual-element V-shaped cat-whisker or an equivalent blade on the vertical stabilizer or upper fuselage. Belly blades are transponders, cabin loops are used for ADF, and wing-tip helicals are not standard VOR practice.

Extended Coverage 1.clue: Beyond the four-digit squawk, a Mode C reply adds this piece of data.
choices: The aircraft's pressure altitude referenced to 29.92 inHg, from the altitude encoder
The aircraft's current GPS latitude and longitude
The local altimeter setting in inches of mercury selected by the pilot
The pilot's voice transmissions on the tuned ATC frequency
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: Mode C adds pressure altitude — derived from the altitude encoder and referenced to 29.92 inHg — to the Mode A identification. GPS coordinates are an ADS-B Out function. The local altimeter setting stays with the pilot; ATC corrects on the ground. Voice traffic is on a separate COM radio.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: Mode C transponders, in addition to Mode A, automatically transmit:

Extended Coverage 1.clue: This federal aviation regulation is the specific rule that requires testing and inspection of an aircraft transponder system.
choices: FAR 91.207 (ELT inspection)
FAR 91.413 (transponder test and inspection)
FAR 91.411 (altimeter and static system test)
FAR 43.3 (maintenance authorization)
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: FAR 91.413 is the transponder-specific regulation and requires the test every 24 calendar months. FAR 91.207 applies to ELTs. FAR 91.411 covers altimeter and static-system tests (and the altitude encoder). FAR 43.3 addresses who may perform maintenance, not what must be tested.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: What regulation specifically requires testing of the transponder system?

Extended Coverage 1.clue: An airborne lightning detection system such as Stormscope is built for this purpose.
choices: To physically protect the airframe from a direct lightning strike in flight
To detect thunderstorm electrical discharges and show positions for avoidance
To ground the airframe and prevent static electricity from building up in flight
To shield every onboard avionics box from external electromagnetic interference
Extended Coverage 1.explanation: Lightning detection systems (Stormscope, Strikefinder) are passive receivers that sense the EM pulses from thunderstorm discharges and plot their positions on a cockpit display. They are a weather-avoidance aid, not structural protection, grounding, or EMI shielding — those functions belong to bonding, static wicks, and enclosure design respectively.
Extended Coverage 1.targetCaetStem: Lightning Detection systems in aircraft are designed to:

Extended Coverage 2.clue: The navigation data delivered to the cockpit by DME equipment is this.
choices: The magnetic bearing from the DME station
Slant-range distance in nautical miles to the ground station
The current weather conditions at the DME site
The active runway in use at the destination airport
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: DME provides slant-range distance — the straight-line distance from the aircraft to the ground station, in nautical miles. Bearing comes from VOR. Weather is broadcast separately (ATIS, AWOS). Runway information is on the approach chart or from ATIS, not DME.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: DME (Distance Measuring Equipment) provides pilots with:

Extended Coverage 2.clue: For the best performance of a VHF communication antenna on a GA aircraft, this is the ideal mounting approach.
choices: Inside the cockpit near the pilot for easy inspection access
Fuselage top or bottom with clear line of sight and ground plane
On the inside surface of the windshield for line-of-sight propagation
Attached to the propeller hub for improved signal radiation in flight
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: A VHF comm antenna (118–137 MHz) performs best fuselage-mounted (top or bottom) with an unobstructed view, a solid ground plane beneath, and proper spacing from other antennas. Interior, propeller, and windshield locations lack those requirements.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: For optimal performance of a VHF communication antenna on a general aviation aircraft, it should ideally be mounted:

Extended Coverage 2.clue: This is the cockpit nav information delivered by a Very High Frequency Omni-directional Range station.
choices: The aircraft's altitude above the VOR site
The distance in nautical miles to the VOR station
The magnetic radial (bearing) from the VOR station to the aircraft
A voice broadcast of current weather at the station
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: VOR supplies bearing — the magnetic radial from the station to the aircraft. Distance is a DME function (often collocated with VOR). Altitude is not a VOR parameter. Weather broadcasts are delivered by HIWAS, ATIS, or AWOS, not by VOR navigation.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: VOR (Very High Frequency Omni-Directional Range) navigation provides pilots with:

Extended Coverage 2.clue: Fitting a new digital transponder antenna, this is the single practice that protects RF performance.
choices: Belly mount with airframe bonding, torqued connectors, and L-band separation
Substitute the specified RF connectors with whatever is available in the shop drawer
Route the antenna coax cable through a hot exhaust duct to evaporate internal moisture
Leave the antenna base unsealed so any condensation inside can freely evaporate out
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: Transponder antennas operate at 1030/1090 MHz and require a belly location with a solid ground plane, torqued approved connectors for the 50-ohm match, and antenna separation to prevent L-band coupling. Hot ducts, wrong connectors, and unsealed bases all degrade RF and mechanical integrity.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: Installing a new digital transponder antenna, which step ensures good performance?

Extended Coverage 2.clue: A VOR ramp test set, used on the ramp against an installed VOR, is typically employed to carry out this task.
choices: Transmitting a simulated VOR signal so the receiver can be verified at selectable radials
Measuring the aircraft's battery voltage while the avionics bus is energized
Performing the FAR 91.171 in-flight VOR accuracy check from the ramp
Physically rotating the aircraft's VOR antenna through 360 degrees to verify mechanical travel
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: A VOR ramp test set radiates a low-power simulated VOR signal, and the technician dials radials to verify the receiver indicates them correctly with appropriate flag and TO/FROM behavior. It does not rotate antennas, measure battery voltage, or replace the in-flight VOR check required by FAR 91.171.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: Ground testing of a VOR navigation system typically involves using a VOR ramp test set to:

Extended Coverage 2.clue: When integrating an audio panel with other avionics, this factor matters most for clear audio and minimum interference.
choices: Clean audio ground, shielded cables per manual, routing away from noise
Use the largest-gauge wire available for every audio line to reduce resistance
Mount the audio panel as close to the alternator as practical for short leads
Use unshielded twisted-pair cable throughout the audio installation to save weight
Extended Coverage 2.explanation: Audio paths are low-level and extremely sensitive to ground loops and induced noise. The dominant factor is proper grounding and shielding — a single-point audio ground, shielded cables terminated per the installation manual, and routing away from alternators, strobes, and heavy current feeders.
Extended Coverage 2.targetCaetStem: When integrating an audio panel with other avionics, which factor is most important for ensuring clear audio and minimal interference?

final.clue: A Mode S transponder differs from Mode A/C by featuring unique selective addressing. How many bits are in a discrete Mode S ICAO aircraft address?
choices: 12-Bit
24-Bit
32-Bit
64-Bit
final.explanation: Every Mode S equipped aircraft is assigned a unique 24-bit ICAO address, hardcoded or strapped into the transponder, allowing over 16 million distinct addresses.

final.clue: A VOR receiver determines bearing to a station by measuring the phase difference between a reference phase signal and this type of signal.
choices: A variable directional phase
A paired DME pulse pair
A 1090 MHz reply
An ILS localizer tone
final.explanation: VORs transmit a 30Hz reference signal (omnidirectional) and a 30Hz variable signal (sweeping directional). The receiver compares their phase difference to determine the specific radial.

final.clue: The Wide Area Augmentation System (WAAS) provides GPS corrections to the aircraft via this specific method.
choices: VHF datalink from ground stations
Geostationary satellites transmitting on
Cellular LTE networks
Mode S Extended Squitter
final.explanation: WAAS ground stations calculate GPS error corrections, uplink them to geostationary (GEO) satellites, which broadcast the corrections back to aircraft using the standard GPS L1 frequency.
